appreciate the kind words.

feel like oftentimes people miss out on that.

you.

would say, like, definitely culture and values are important.

and then like the I'll go super fast because I kind of go through the story and the pitch that that's recorded.
Right.

But but kind of like how we started, I think was really unique, which was, you know, from the beginning, when even when I was at IBM, my goal was always to just how do you get brands and companies to be more like publishers?
And the way to do that and kind of the realization was like IBM was spending over a billion dollars a year in marketing.
And so if they took just a fraction of that, they could literally build multiple New York times.
They could literally acquire the top two or three media companies in the planet if they wanted to, every year, not once, like every year, right?
And they could literally acquire a bunch of influencers.

They could kind of do whatever they want, but somehow they choose to, you know, spend millions and millions of dollars in content syndication and ads and channels, you know, at the expense of actually building their own audience, right?
And so the site got sunset recently, but it's called securityintelligence.com, where I literally just said, hey, why don't we become the publication for the space we're in?
And then I just started convincing people to publish under it.
And then I started to build distribution.
So I built a newsletter.
And then I repeated that playbook over and over again in my career in different ways, right?
Like Tech Beacon, I built this contributor network that had over a thousand contributors and grew organically.
And I just remember really clearly, like, at HP when I was building Tech Beacon, you know, it's just simple.
It was just like, hey.
How do we talk to people in the industry and the people that we're trying to serve and understand the questions they have along their journey around those topics?
And how do we just go and then research those questions and figure out what's missing from the answers that exist?
And then how do we just systematically and over and over again become by far the best and most useful answer to those questions?
And then how do you just do that every single week, you know, and then just keep doing that over and over again?
What do you need?
Well, you need to have a lot of experts that are publishing for you.
OK, let's go recruit those.
OK, you need to find ways to find the right opportunities systematically.
And then you need ways to make sure that you're handing assignments to people that tell them what they need to cover to make sure they're better than the other answers that are out there.
You know, and those are all like the first principle things that just worked.
We grew to over a million visitors a month, you know, which for a technical publication is really good.
We grew a newsletter, 200,000 subscribers, and it was just like the same thing.
And every single week, I just remember my editors sitting down on me going, hey, I don't care about any metrics.
All I care about is that we grew organic traffic this week by just a little bit.
That's all I cared about.
It's like every week getting a little bit better.
And then you see the chart over here, right?
Like, this is an actual chart, you know, there's lots of net accounts on some things that have viral, but like over the time, it just consistently grew and it became extremely high ROI, right?
Like, that's kind of like the premise of what we've done.

And then at DeepGram, as I started to kind of take a lot of these concepts and build it into workflows, then I took the editors that I would hire, writers that I would hire, and I said, hey, here's my thought process.
This is what has worked.
Come plug into the system, but still have this oversight and use your judgment and use your understanding of clients, right, to do really well.
We did that with DeepGram, and I think the thing that we really need to make sure we still capture was a couple of things, right?
Like, we were taking some risk, and we were being really aggressive, but the whole idea is, like, it's not for AI just to give us better margins or enable us to automate a couple of things.
AI should enable us to take better bets, higher quality bets, and more bets more often so that we can learn faster, right?

Like, if that's not happening, and we're just publishing a few posts, and we're not looking at the data, and we're not seeing what's working, and we're not looking at the signals, we're not doubling down on what's working, like, then it defeats the purpose.
The purpose here is not for us to just, like, we publish five articles a week, see you later, right?
Like, it's really for us to just accelerate the learning process to understand what's going to work and double down on what's working.
And when we did that at DeepGram really, really fast with 24x traffic and had these amazing results, right?
But what's really cool, and I think why we've done so well is...
The company, there's a lot to fix.
There's a lot to improve.
But it's because I just did these workshops where I spent like five hours just teaching people exactly what I had built.
Right.
And I think people pay to come to those.
And so when you spend that much time, it just becomes like so in your face of like, these are the pain points.
These are the things that people need help with.
But also, it just builds an insane amount of trust.
Like even to this day, like I get messages from people like, hey, I've been following you for years.
I'm like, how?
Like, I don't even know how.
I'm like, you know, it's just like, oh, I've been paying attention.

Like, because, you know, there's just so much noise and crap out there that anytime someone shares something that's kind of like legit based on like real learnings and based on like real experience, it just like stands out.
Right.
And it's not because we're trying to hook people.
It's just because people are like, okay, like this is legit.
Like this is like, this is the real deal.
Right.
And I think that's also why like in our hiring process, you're seeing like we're trying to find the best of the best.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
It doesn't matter where they live.
It doesn't matter, like, their background.
What matters is, like, they know their stuff and they have the right approach, right, and the right mindset.
And so we started to help customers in seeing a lot of results.

And I think, like, since then, there's just been a lot of validation of, like, AI plus experts being the right approach versus, like, trying to just automate things or, you know, completely ignore AI, you know.

And I think the reality today is that, like, a lot of these companies, like, they're just spread so thin and they don't want to go hire a head of content, a head of SEO, writers, editors, right, like a production assistant to stage things in the CMS and publish it, a graphic designer.
Like, you know, you do all of that and then you spend hundreds of thousands of dollars and, you know, you're six months in and you haven't published or done anything or seen any results, right?
So that's why, like, we focus on outcome.
But one thing I'll mention is, like, the size of the opportunity.
There's a notion on this, but, you know, SEMrush is a tool that a lot of us use and have used.
They have about 25,000 customers that would be what we consider, you know, ideal for us, right?
Like mid-market and enterprise.
And so if those 25,000 customers, which are already paying for a tool that doesn't actually do the work for them, just gives them insights, right?
We're paying us our average deal size, we would be at $3.8 billion in annual revenue.
So just think about that for a second, right?
These are 25,000 companies, it's not a lot, this is not even the entire team, that are already paying $10,000, $20,000, $30,000, $40,000 a year for a tool that doesn't actually do the work for them.
And we've already proven that people are willing to pay $9,000 to $40,000 a month to get what we're doing, right?
And so, like, to me, like, that's as clear as day, right?
Like, we need to just continue to make our process more repeatable, help companies.
Be aggressive, continue to build a reputation and continue to help customers win and then tell those stories.
And that's it.
Like, you know, it's that simple.

But it doesn't stop there because like our services, there's only one aspect of our potential growth and what we're doing, you know, but like that aspect alone can be $3 billion a year in revenue, right?
And so if that's all we were doing for the rest of our existence, we could be, you know, an amazing, amazing generational company, right?

But I think what's really cool is that like when you think about like the needs in the market and what people, our customers are asking for, it goes way beyond, you know, editorial or programmatic SEO.
You know, people want to help like on creative and graphics and long form content and lead magnets and help out and newsletters and prospecting and research.
Like just last week, a couple weeks ago, we had the equity partners.
They have $100 billion in companies that they own their private equity.
Asking us, hey, can you expose your platform to us to power all our sales processes for all 90 companies we own?
We have customers asking for it, right?
And so anyways, this gives you a little bit of a view.

So as we started to scale this and these workflows, we started running to every issue imaginable, right?
And part of it is because people are trying to do what is best in code, but they're trying to take it away from code and trying to do tools that you drag and drop, right?
That just don't scale.
And so we were using some of those low-code tools like AeroOps, NAN, Gumloop, all of those, right?
And what has started to happen is like all of them started to break, but initially last year, we thought we were just going to adapt these workflows for people and hand it to them as a turnkey.
We'll

And so we got to a point where it's just like, okay, if you have the most high intent people, they're willing to commit the time, come to my workshops and spend all those money to come to it.
And then I give them all the tips and everything on how to do this thing.
And then even after then, only one in every 100 actually go and spend an extra thousand hours actually figuring this out.
This is not going to scale.
That is not where the future is.
The future is not going to be people spending thousands and thousands of hours.
There's going to be those, and they're going to have an unfair advantage for a little while.
But I think for us, what we realize is just the way the obsession is, right, where it's low code, whereas code actually works.
And so for us, like a lot of what we build is kind of on this premise that like we.
This paradigm shift, right?

And just kind of, and this is probably like if you take nothing away from this session, this is probably the most important piece, which is if you think about the productivity of your standard software developer engineer over the last 10 years, how much more productive is someone using Cursor and GitHub and CI and CD and all the latest and greatest tools today as an engineer than they were 10 years ago?
And that is insane.
There are at least 100 more productive.

Better communication tools like Slack, you know?

I'll give you half of my salary if you took this away from me.

Sure, I'll Notion.
want to take away Notion?
I'll use Google Docs.
You want to take away Slack?

And then if you think about like how you've gone about doing your own work, and if you had all those thought processes, distilling code and workflows and all those activities and all the change logs and everything and all that data, like I would pay a year's salary for that data for me, for my data on that, right?
But imagine if companies had that.
Imagine Accenture had every single workflow, how every job to be done distilled into files and distilled into code that you can learn from, right?

And part of the reason why AI does so well with code is because code is structured and you can see the diff and you can see if it's tested, you can test it if it's running or not, if it's what it's doing, right?
There's frameworks, there's structures, you can learn from it.
And that's ultimately like what we're building underneath the hood, which is starting with outcomes and almost creating...

Config file for work, you know, and then having these apps and reusable apps, you will, right, that are modulars and tools that AI can kind of learn from and then an ability to run that massive scale, right?
And then an observability layer, and most importantly, a learn layer and a task hub, if you will, right?
Like how does experts collaborate with these workflows, but also how can experts and humans review the different tasks?
And then you can build evaluations on it.
And over time, the idea is like, we're learning about you as the expert doing the work and what you're best at.
But also we're taking that and making sure our workflows and our outputs get better and better and better.
But then over time, we can also learn like what tasks and what reviews to assign to whom, right?
And all of that data, the byproduct of all that data is frontier data that can be used to train models as well for those specific things, right?
And so it's like this confluence.
Like outcomes that are expert led and, you know, collaborated with experts and then backing into the technology versus starting with the technology and and then trying to get to the outcomes.
Right.

And and I think like the last thing I'll say and, you know, this is not ready for Showtime externally, but like this is kind of how we think about our product roadmap, you know, and the learn engine is the thing that when we show investors makes them like their eyes open up like, holy crap, I had no idea.

This is what you all are working on, you know, because in some ways it's like AI workflows represented in code and the ability to learn from every run, every workflow, every interaction and every output.
Right.
And and so so that's a little bit of our vision and and then like the phases of our growth.
Right.
And kind of how we're about it is is ultimately like, you know, right now we're in the space one where it's content and growth and and then building our internal platform.
And then.
And continue to validate this, right?
And then our goal is to hit 100 plus clients this year, 23 million in ARR, and then potentially raise a series B, and then go really, really aggressive on expanding it to different services.

You know, like, I mean, we already have our workflows that we're using for recruiting, you know, people are already asking for sales, there's like, this is highly adaptable, but also starting to monetize and expose our APIs, our platform.
Think of, like, cursor for knowledge work, if you will.
And so that's our vision and our roadmap.
Hopefully it gets you as pumped and excited as I am, you know?
And so with that, again, like, a lot of this is written in more details in the docs as well, but hopefully this gives you good context, but give me the hard questions.
I really love the idea of the learn engine, Marcel.
Like, that's the ability to essentially layer on...

I don't mean to sound cynical, but basically the ability to layer on every employee knowledge base on top of itself and create these multiple layers of learning for an institution, that's just mind-blowing, man.
That's revolutionary.
I can see why they would be getting excited about that because it's no longer necessarily like a knowledge loss when an employee or someone leaves or retires or whatever.
It's actually building.
It becomes the next C-level for change.
So that's really, wow, .
How do you extract expertise, right?
And in knowledge work, it would be like the equivalent of documentation, standard operating procedures.
It would be the comments back and forth, the thought process along the way, the conversations, right?
Like, and when you think about code, that's all-
Like, you got docs, got repos, you got, like, pull requests, you got, like, you know.

Yeah, and I think that's what's so brilliant because, again, you can have a lot of this institutional knowledge, but it's usually, like, in a confluence or scattered across, like, again, multiple projects, documents, whatever.
It's there, but the code makes it accessible and then makes it programmable, which is, again,  phenomenal.
And then you can run it and then you can decide what to have human interventions and to whom and how and, you know, and learn from all of that, right?
And then over time, like, for those of you that, whether familiar or not, right, like, fine-tuning is so easy these days, right?
Like, so you can fine-tune models, you can train models too, but there's also, like, distilling models, so you can take a bigger model.

And if you have the right data, you can actually distill it to be very specific towards something, you know, and so that's a lot of what some of these models from, like, China and others have done, being able to catch up.
And even exceed expectations is kind of how they've done it in a more compute efficient way.
so with this approach, every module, if you will, every workflow can eventually become a fine-tuned model, can be a task-specific model or a domain-specific model, right?
Or a company-specific model as well.
Not to mention that a lot of these APIs and workflows, people want it as a service, right?
So if you think about our fact-checker, people are like, I want that as an API.
If you have a network of amazing experts, people want human reviews as an API, right?
Like, our deep researcher is actually better than OpenAI's deep researcher, in my opinion, for researching topics, you know?
And so, like, all those things can be APIs that people want access to, you know?
And so, but the amazing thing is that because we're services, we have this crazy unfair advantage, right?
And so the world, most of
Well, they're starting to catch up to it, but they think of services as a bad thing, right?
Low margin, hard to scale.

We think of services as a positive thing because every single piece of technology that we're building, everything we're doing is to solve something we're already delivering as a service, which means there's zero sunken cost.
Like every single minute of an engineer's time or any of our time is to deliver something we're already getting paid for.
And that already has product market fit because someone's already paying for it.
So it's literally like a product market fit engine services, right?
Like if you do it in the right way.
And that's why like we're so efficient.
By the end of the year, we'll be 76% still gross margin.
And so like our margins will be a little bit lower this quarter, next quarter, because like we're overhiring to make sure we have a lot of capacity and things like that.
But it's huge, huge potential.
What else?
What other questions other folks have?
I don't have one question, but I have a meeting in two minutes, maybe for another time.
ask?
What

And, you know, that kind of thing.
Yeah, I mean, we've done a lot of like content refreshes at a bigger scale, right?
I think the learn engine is the piece that we really need to get better at, right?
Like, so if you have a pixel on someone's site or you have access to that data, which is like visitor data, right?
Like how to engage in a data site.
If you have access to a search console and you, and then you have access to all these APIs like perplexity and open AIs to understand like who's showing up in these searches.
You have both the external and the internal data to understand like the inputs are driving the right outputs, right?
And what actions are driving the right reactions, if you will, and there.
And so over time, you can learn from it, right?
There's just like a lot.
about
And so, like, I think Google itself doesn't even know exactly how to evolve things.

And so the bottom line, though, is, like, solve for humans, not for machines has always been a principle that works, which is, like, if a human reads this thing that you're trying to serve and goes, this is so effing useful, like, that's the bar.
That should always be the bar, you know?
And then if you do that, all these other tools and services and things are just trying to solve for that.
They're trying to solve for the best answer, the most useful answer, and the best experiences, you know?
So, like, we should focus on that more than, like, what is Google's algorithm trying to do, you know?
Fair enough.
That's a really good answer.

Yay.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Onboard new team members and share GrowthX's vision, strategy, and product roadmap.](https://fathom.video/share/_boFydEn76HzPDR5guWSdkCgv1JcxHU_?tab=summary&timestamp=5.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [GrowthX's core strategy: Become a publisher-like entity for clients, systematically creating best-in-class content](https://fathom.video/share/_boFydEn76HzPDR5guWSdkCgv1JcxHU_?tab=summary&timestamp=512.0)
  - [Massive market opportunity: $3.8B potential annual revenue from just 25,000 ideal customers](https://fathom.video/share/_boFydEn76HzPDR5guWSdkCgv1JcxHU_?tab=summary&timestamp=930.0)
  - [Unique approach: AI + human experts, focusing on outcomes and learning from every interaction](https://fathom.video/share/_boFydEn76HzPDR5guWSdkCgv1JcxHU_?tab=summary&timestamp=738.0)
  - [Revolutionary "learn engine": Captures institutional knowledge, enables continuous improvement, and creates potential for fine-tuned AI models](https://fathom.video/share/_boFydEn76HzPDR5guWSdkCgv1JcxHU_?tab=summary&timestamp=1332.0)

## Topics

### Company Origin and Strategy

  - [Marcel's background: Built content strategies at IBM, HP (Tech Beacon)](https://fathom.video/share/_boFydEn76HzPDR5guWSdkCgv1JcxHU_?tab=summary&timestamp=512.0)
  - [Core approach: Become the best publisher in clients' spaces](https://fathom.video/share/_boFydEn76HzPDR5guWSdkCgv1JcxHU_?tab=summary&timestamp=512.0)
  - [Key elements: Expert contributors, systematic opportunity identification, high-quality content creation](https://fathom.video/share/_boFydEn76HzPDR5guWSdkCgv1JcxHU_?tab=summary&timestamp=623.0)
  - [Results: Consistent organic growth, high ROI (e.g., Tech Beacon: 1M+ monthly visitors, 200k newsletter subscribers)](https://fathom.video/share/_boFydEn76HzPDR5guWSdkCgv1JcxHU_?tab=summary&timestamp=656.0)

### Market Opportunity and Differentiation

  - [Target market: 25,000 ideal customers (mid-market & enterprise) from SEMrush's user base](https://fathom.video/share/_boFydEn76HzPDR5guWSdkCgv1JcxHU_?tab=summary&timestamp=913.0)
  - [Potential annual revenue: $3.8B if all converted to GrowthX's average deal size](https://fathom.video/share/_boFydEn76HzPDR5guWSdkCgv1JcxHU_?tab=summary&timestamp=930.0)
  - [Current pricing: $9,000 to $40,000 per month for services](https://fathom.video/share/_boFydEn76HzPDR5guWSdkCgv1JcxHU_?tab=summary&timestamp=951.0)
  - [Expansion potential: Creative, graphics, lead magnets, newsletters, sales enablement](https://fathom.video/share/_boFydEn76HzPDR5guWSdkCgv1JcxHU_?tab=summary&timestamp=1000.0)

### Product Vision and Roadmap

  - [Problem: Existing low-code tools don't scale for complex workflows](https://fathom.video/share/_boFydEn76HzPDR5guWSdkCgv1JcxHU_?tab=summary&timestamp=1048.0)
  - [Solution: Building a code-based platform for knowledge work](https://fathom.video/share/_boFydEn76HzPDR5guWSdkCgv1JcxHU_?tab=summary&timestamp=1260.0)
  - [Key components:](https://fathom.video/share/_boFydEn76HzPDR5guWSdkCgv1JcxHU_?tab=summary&timestamp=1260.0)
    1.  Outcome-focused config files for work
    2.  Modular, reusable apps and tools
    3.  Observability layer
    4.  Learn engine and task hub
  - [Benefits: Increased productivity, institutional knowledge capture, potential for AI model fine-tuning](https://fathom.video/share/_boFydEn76HzPDR5guWSdkCgv1JcxHU_?tab=summary&timestamp=1316.0)
